data.title: Verizon: High-Yield Cash Flow Powerhouse Poised For A Major Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Verizon (VZ) is one of the largest US telecom companies, recently expanding through a couple of acquisitions and announcing a CEO change that already came with a bold move and improved vision.

Despite internal and macro improvements and still very strong cash flow generation, the stock is still trading at very attractive levels, justifying a Strong Buy rating in my opinion that’s backed by a strong and sustainable ~6.7% dividend yield.

Verizon’s Q3 came with yet another consecutive quarter of consumer retail postpaid losses, although we see it slow down significantly, while the net adds in broadband and everything else remained relatively pressured, albeit positive, helping Verizon reach a 5.4% YoY increase in wireless retail gross adds in Q3.

Still, from a FCF point of view, we see significant improvements despite the higher CAPEX, with a 16.7% YoY increase in free cash flow during the quarter and 9% increase over the first 9 months of 2025. The company’s reiterated guidance forecasts FCF between $19.5 billion to $20.5 billion in 2025, placing them at a P/FCF ratio of around 8.67 based on the midpoint, although this is not that impressive for them at least in recent history.

Recently, Verizon announced naming a new CEO, with former PayPal (PYPL) CEO Dan Schulman taking over and already announcing significant changes, with a new vision aiming at better customer experiences, cost savings and investing in growth. As planned, Verizon’s major layoffs have reportedly begun, planning to cut over 13,000 jobs and move 179 stores to franchise operations, planning “significant cost savings across all aspects” of the business.

A similar CEO transition did wonders for AT&T’s (T) stock a couple of years ago, and I’m happily holding my position that’s up 75% in a bit over two years now. More importantly, this type of move can also be one of several important catalysts for Verizon, especially since the business is still solid from a fundamental perspective, with very strong cash flows despite the recent weakness.

Financially, based on Verizon’s latest report, we see a better financial position than the market suggests. The current assets are generally always below their current liabilities (potentially generating short-term debt costs that come with higher interest rates now), while the long-term debt remains very significant.

However, the timing of this debt remains a crucial aspect to consider. Sure, it generates a ton of interest, but they don’t have to repay it all at once (they don’t really have to repay it since they just refinance it). Verizon’s debt is spread over several decades and a majority of it comes at a manageable interest rate, which is far from a major issue that puts them at risk in their current state.

Dividends & Buybacks

Verizon currently pays a ~6.7% yield that costs about $11.68 billion per year, for a payout ratio of roughly 58.4% at the midpoint of this year’s FCF guidance, making it very sustainable. This dividend has been increased for 19 consecutive years following the recent increase in September 2025, and I see no reason for them to cut it any time soon unless they plan a massive fundamental reorganization.

Similar to AT&T, Verizon could also go for some buybacks on top to sustain this turnaround, especially since they’d save a lot (~6.7%) by buying their own stock. Ideally, they’d repurchase some of their 7-8% debt alongside the stock, potentially saving a lot in interest and dividends paid, freeing up a lot of cash in the future. As a note, Verizon has a buyback program for up to 100 million shares since early 2020, but is not actively repurchasing anything, for now.

Risks & Opportunities

Rate cuts will be one of the most important catalysts for Verizon from many points of view. Of course, there’s the obvious effect on their massive amount of debt that will continue to get refinanced/rolled over, with these cuts lowering the company’s interest expenses (~$6.58 billion YTD) over time and potentially unlocking a lot of cash flow on top of the company’s own initiatives. This can then be used to repay debt, do buybacks, invest, etc., so it’s not hard to see things improve significantly for VZ given enough time in a lower-rate environment.

Despite the relative defensiveness of this industry, this can also be great for the final consumer by increasing discretionary budgets. Meanwhile, we could see a rise in business activity as well, meaning potential to see more clients enter the market or existing ones expanding, so VZ still stands to benefit from this.

Also, these rate cuts should have an effect on treasuries, with rate cuts being a big factor that can push the risk-free yield down again (not the only, but a major one), potentially making high-yield dividend stocks like Verizon more attractive once again. As we can see, it’s not only the correlation between interest rates and treasuries that can be seen with the naked eye, but the company’s dividend (among many others) is also affected.

That’s because when treasuries pay 4% or even 5%, why would the market go for a company like Verizon for the same yield and higher risk, just to hopefully get a premium from stock price appreciation? This makes the opportunity cost shift significantly compared to back when these yields were ~2% or even lower, and the opposite should happen once rates are cut.

Also, with Powell’s term ending in May 2026, we could be looking at yet another opportunity for this. Even though the Fed Chair is just one out of twelve votes in the Federal Open Market Committee, this person is still a very important spokesperson for the US economy as a whole, as their views and opinions carry a ton of influence for the general market. So, if Trump now manages to name someone more obedient this time, there is potential to see a lot more confidence in the Fed’s next moves, although it also carries the risk of being forced to listen to Trump rather than doing what the economy needs at the time.

But again, these improvements and the potential recovery won’t come overnight. It can take years to see these lower rates reflected in the company’s financials or until their customers (people/businesses) would benefit from them. The fear around delays also rose while the US Government went through its longest shutdown in history, leading to even more uncertainty although some Fed officials signaled the possibility of a December cut - but we should know for sure soon, as the Fed’s next meeting is scheduled for December 9 and 10.

Still, despite playing in what we can call an oligopoly, the competition remains a big issue for Verizon, being accentuated by the quality of their services, which ultimately limits their growth potential significantly. The issue is that despite the very high entry barrier needed to play in this market, there are also other big players joining various segments - such as Amazon (AMZN) or Google (GOOG) - which can easily compete with these players.

This growing attention from these tech titans comes as the US telecom market comes with massive margins, with Verizon itself being a great example, with significant returns and generally double-digit margins. These could also be potential buyers, although I don’t believe any company would be allowed to buy something like Verizon, although this can offer more potential buyers in case the company wants to divest some more assets going further.

The advantage they have over pharma like CVS (CVS) or Walgreens [WBA] and why something like Amazon joining the market likely won’t be detrimental is ultimately the high margins. If competition gets much tougher and their oligopoly is challenged, these companies have plenty of room to cut margins or especially improve their services, while the high CAPEX may not be the best fit for some of these tech giants given their already ramped-up AI/Cloud investments.

Somewhat related to this and the high levels of debt is the fundamental “issue” of this type of company: having to follow its peers into investment cycles. As we’ve seen a few years ago with 5G and the C-Band spectrum auction (where Verizon was the biggest spender), these companies can’t afford to sit and let their competitors get ready for the next big thing and hope to catch up later.

With companies already working on 6G, data center expansion and many AI applications - see the recent announcement from NVIDIA (NVDA) and Nokia (NOK), for example - this type of massive CAPEX is basically guaranteed to come in the future, leaving little breathing room for deleveraging. Sure, the company could afford to repay some debt every year, but it’s not as detrimental as the market makes it to be - compared to being left behind in the face of a potentially massive trend.

As a note, Verizon also made some bold moves recently, agreeing to buy Frontier Communications (FYBR) in an all-cash deal valued at $20 billion in late 2024, which is a costly bet on fiber internet and expanding the company’s reach into home internet, with potential to expand as a much bigger combined company. Currently, that’s about 20 times Frontier’s annualized net cash from operations, although we can see that the company is investing massive amounts of money in CAPEX and expanding significantly over the past years, so there’s definitely potential for Verizon to expand it even more, potentially saving from synergies including better costs of debt.

The deal is expected to close in early 2026, and we’ve also seen a move in a similar direction a few months ago when Verizon announced buying Starry, a fixed wireless internet provider, also expected to close around Q1 2026.

Despite the deal happening before his time, Verizon’s new CEO pointed out how this is aligned with his view during the Q3 Earnings Report, with his recent moves being a next step into his fundamental trajectory change:

As you know, we're significantly investing in cyber and in the power of convergence with our acquisition of Frontier, which we expect to close early next year. Verizon has deep strength and incredible potential, but the blunt truth is we haven't captured the customer growth opportunities this strong foundation enables. Verizon is at a critical inflection point. ... With that foundation firmly in place, our next chapter is about serving and delighting customers by building the industry's best overall value proposition and the best customer experience on top of it. ... The only way we can drive sustainable value for our shareholders is by significantly raising our game and winning responsibly in the market. This is about financially disciplined growth, winning with the right customers at the right economics. Volume growth and profitability growth can go and will go hand-in-hand. We are fully committed to achieving both simultaneously.

Meanwhile, the long-term trends like digitalization, access to faster technologies, etc. should continue, while trends like AI, data centers and the others mentioned before should continue to offer opportunities. We’ve even seen Verizon struck a deal with Amazon’s AWS to build new high-capacity, low-latency fiber network infrastructure for the tech giant’s data centers.

Overall, there are several potential catalysts that can benefit Verizon, while their recent investments and the high room for quality improvements can help them achieve better results in the future. However, plenty of risks remain, as the rising competition can take advantage of Verizon’s turnaround or transition efforts to capture even more market share, making it a tough business for the new CEO to handle today.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse set of peers including bigger direct ones like AT&T or T-Mobile (TMUS) but also Charter Communications (CHTR), Comcast (CMCSA) Lumen Technologies (LUMN) and EchoStar (SATS), we see these valuations being somewhere in the middle of the general market and very similar to AT&T’s, while some of the smaller peers could represent buying opportunities - as they did recently with Frontier or Starry and AT&T did with United States Cellular spectrum assets. VZ also pays the highest yield in today’s market, well above AT&T and Comcast and many times above T-Mobile.

However, these numbers have their limitations, as there isn’t really any metric that can reflect the “on the ground” customer opinion or performance of the company, especially given that Verizon's public image is currently poor. Again, this can be improved and the new CEO could address this, but I just wanted to point out that numbers won’t ever tell us the full story.

Valuation

Starting with Verizon’s expected free cash flow of $20 billion in 2025, let’s assume a 4% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to rate cuts and their cost-saving and reinvestment initiatives (not far from the estimates available here), followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a 7.5% WACC (well above my estimated ~5.9% based on a ~7.3% cost of equity and ~4.3% cost of debt), with $162.75 billion in net debt, $1.3 billion in non-controlling interests and 4.23 billion shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This gets us to an enterprise value of $419.15 billion, an equity value of $255.1 billion and an intrinsic value of $60.31 per share. This may seem like much, but it would bring VZ back to a dividend yield of 4.57%, which is in line with their levels from the pre-hike times.

Conclusions

I rate Verizon a Strong Buy now thanks to their significant turnaround potential in an improving environment, backed by their strong cash flow generation and new CEO’s initiatives. This will continue to present risks, but the upside coming from a potential turnaround, very strong and sustainable dividend plus the flexibility they have even after paying them greatly offset them, in my opinion.

data.title: MTN Group Offers Opportunity, But It's Not An Easy Pick
data.description:
If you want to talk about growth potential stemming from a combination of population growth, household income improvement from low bases, and tech-driven disintermediation, it’s hard to do better than Africa and companies that serve African consumers like MTN Group (OTCPK:MTNOY)(OTCPK:MTNOF). With meaningful population growth in multiple markets, improvements in household income/affordability (though certainly not consistent across all markets), and still-meaningful opportunities to leverage data and financial services growth, MTN Group would certainly seem to offer a lot of potential. It's never been that simple with MTN Group, though, and the shares have lost about 30% of their value since my last update on the company, more or less matching South African rival Vodacom (OTCPK:VDMCY) but lagging others like Orange (OTC:ORANY) and Bharti Airtel (and Airtel Africa (OTCPK:AARTY)) over that time, with ongoing and familiar issues like currency devaluation, squabbles with governments, and competitive challenges.

MTN Group does seem undervalued in some respects, but investors shouldn’t assume this is “easy money.” The company’s reporting is more opaque than I’d like, disputes with governments in countries where it operates are all too common, and even if things were perfect on those fronts, there’s the not-so-simple issue of trying to track a business that operates across a giant continent and has roughly 600 million inhabitants across the markets in which it operates.

Recent Trends Are Positive, Though South Africa Remains Challenging

MTN’s most recent financial update (third quarter results) was encouraging on balance, as the company continues to recover from a 2024 marred by currency devaluation in Nigeria (though underlying constant currency results were fine).

Revenue rose about 29% in the third quarter, bringing like-for-like constant-currency revenue growth up to about 20% for the year to date, including almost 23% growth in service revenue.

South Africa remains a notable laggard, with a small year-over-year decline in revenue (down about 3%) and just over 1% growth in service revenue. The main issue here continues to be stiff competition (primarily with Vodacom) in the prepaid business that makes up about three-quarters of the customer base. Nigeria continues to rebound strongly (up almost 63% on 47% ARPU growth), and Ghana continues to thrive (up 30%), while Uganda continues to grow at a more sedate pace (up about 14%).

Data revenue continues to grow well, up 35% in constant currency and offsetting 11% growth in voice revenue. Fintech revenue likewise continues to be a major growth driver, with 23% constant currency growth this quarter as transaction volume grew 12% and transaction value grew about 58%.

With strong growth in higher-margin data and fintech revenue, margins are re-expanding, with EBITDA up about 39% and margin up 850 bp to 46%. South Africa bucked this trend (down almost 7%, with margin down 150 bp to 34.5%), but Nigeria saw strong margin expansion (up almost 15 pts to 52.9%), and Ghana and Uganda both saw expansion (up 230 bp to 58.1%, and up 210 bp to 54.1%, respectively).

MoMo Continues To Drive Momentum

MTN Group’s financial tech platform MoMo (Mobile Money) has been a key driver for this story for years, and that is unlikely to change anytime soon. An early mover in the space, MTN Group has continued to expand what its platform can do, from relatively simple cash transfers and payments to a range of services that now include loans and insurance products. In a continent where lack of access to banks/bank accounts is a real issue and cross-border migration for work is common, to say nothing of the inflation and security risks of holding substantial amounts of hard local currency, these services have proven hugely popular with MTN Group’s customer base and have played an invaluable role in driving better retention rates for higher-margin customers.

I see no real issues on the horizon to suggest that this growth is about to stop. MTN Group has had to build the business almost literally one country at a time but has slowly been accumulating the necessary approvals to manage it more as a unified enterprise—something that increases the economies of scale and makes the company an even more attractive partner to companies that want to offer services through the platform.

As an example, the company has started rolling out a virtual MasterCard (MA) in three markets (Cote d’Ivoire, Uganda, and Rwanda) that functions as a regular credit card but “lives” within the phone, and I expect similar offerings in the future. With nearly 68 million users (out of around 301 million total subs), this remains an ongoing revenue and margin growth driver.

Compliance Issues Persist

It would take an article (or two) of its own to detail all of the hassles and compliance issues that MTN Group has experienced over the years, and particularly in Nigeria. Suffice it to say, nothing is ever “settled” in that country where MTN Group is concerned. The company is currently appealing a tax liability claim from the federal government and dealing with a claim from the Osun state that the company owes it right of way fees. While the amount of Osun’s claim is small in relative terms (945M naira is less than $1M), the company is understandably wary of setting a precedent of paying unfounded and/or illegitimate claims.

It's not just Nigeria where MTN Group has had issues. The company was fined in Cameroon for service quality issues, and this has been a problem from time to time in other jurisdictions as well (and tough to prevent entirely given the challenges of operating/maintaining infrastructure in some rather remote regions).

More potentially serious, at least from a headline standpoint, is the United States Department of Justice investigation into whether MTN Group “aided and abetted” militia groups in Afghanistan by providing phone service through its Iranian subsidiary. I am also aware of civil cases filed in the U.S. against MTN Group alleging liability for U.S. soldiers injured or killed while serving in Afghanistan, but it’s difficult to find up-to-date information on these (some have been dismissed and others have been allowed to proceed).

It remains to be seen what will become of this. To the best of my knowledge, MTN Group doesn’t have substantial assets in the U.S. that courts could seize, and I find it unlikely that all that many African governments would be eager to cooperate with any enforcement of a U.S. court ruling within their own borders, particularly given recent dust-ups between the U.S. administration and South Africa’s government. Still, it remains at the very least a “headline risk” and something worth monitoring.

The Outlook

As I said in the open, modeling and monitoring MTN Group is a considerable challenge given the number of countries in which it operates and the company-specific dynamics of each business unit. With that, the best you can do is model in accordance with general trends and themes – South Africa remains a challenging market (as it has for some time now), Nigeria continues to offer gaudy growth but a lot of operational risk (whether legal/regulatory or economic), and countries like Ghana continue to offer attractive, relatively “clean” growth stories, underpinned in particular by opportunities in data and fintech revenue growth.

I’m looking for around 10% annualized growth over the next five years, off of an admittedly easier base in FY’24, and longer-term growth of around 7% to 8%. While that may sound low, particularly given how many Africans still live below the poverty line and could one day become data and/or fintech customers, I’d note that the company has grown a little under 11% on an annualized basis over the last 20 years – so it’s not as if I’m calling for a major slowdown. I’d also note that with the entry of companies like Airtel Africa, many markets have become more competitive.

Margins are likewise always a challenge to model beyond the short term, but I do see a path to mid-to-high-40% EBITDA margins in the coming years and low double-digit free cash flow margins. While MTN Group has historically had to spend considerable sums on capex, I do see some moderation there in the future, as a lot of “heavy lifting” was done in 2022 and 2023. All told, I expect low-teens annualized FCF growth over time.

Discounting those cash flows back and applying a pretty robust discount rate to account for the elevated operating risk, I believe the shares are around 15% to 20% undervalued today. Likewise, a 4x multiple on forward EBITDA (low versus many emerging market players, but not unreasonable given heightened operating risk and lower ROIC) suggests around 30% upside.

The Bottom Line

I do think MTN Group is likely undervalued today, and possibly meaningfully so. The “but” is that this is a difficult company to follow, monitor, and model, and there are likely easier opportunities for a 20%-30% gain out there. Still, for those willing to take on the extra work, it is an interesting name leveraged not just to improve internal operational performance but also a lot of long-term growth trends in Africa.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Airbus Deserves To Fly High
data.description:
When it comes to the aviation space, the big behemoth out there is Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY). This is a company that I have known about for several years. But honestly, I haven't really dug deep into it like I have competitor The Boeing Company (BA). Looking at the business today, I see a firm that's growing nicely. Top-line and most bottom line results are expanding. Backlog is growing. And on top of this, while I wouldn't exactly call shares cheap, they are far from expensive and are trading near the lower end of the spectrum compared to other similar firms. For an industry leader that's doing well, I would actually consider the stock attractively priced. But only for those who don't mind paying for high quality. Add on top of this favorable industry conditions that should create even more growth over the next couple of decades, and I see no reason why I shouldn't rate this a ‘buy’.

A necessary disclosure

Before we dive in, it is important to make note that Airbus reports financial results using euros. I, on the other hand, think about things in the form of the US dollar. And I would venture to say that the majority of my readers do the same. Because of this, all of the financial data provided throughout this article, unless otherwise stated, is in US dollars. To accomplish this, I used the current exchange rate and applied it to all windows of time for the company.

Taking a flight with Airbus

If you have done any significant amount of travel, especially outside of the US, odds are that you have flown on an Airbus aircraft. The company is a giant in the commercial aviation space. And most of its revenue comes specifically from providing large passenger and freighter aircraft, as well as related services, to companies in the commercial airline industry. However, there are a couple of other operating segments that it has.

One of these is the Airbus Helicopters segment, which produces both civil and military rotorcraft. Specifically, this part of the company emphasizes intermediate single engine, light twin-engine, medium, and medium-heavy rotorcraft and drones. And then finally, we have its Airbus Defense and Space segment, which might actually be the most interesting to me since I am a space nerd who once considered going to college specifically for theoretical astrophysics. Through this unit, the company produces various products, systems, and services that aim to empower government agencies, businesses, and other institutions to protect civilians and/or to capitalize on the massive opportunity that exists in space.

Honestly, an entire article could be written about these operating segments. But I think you would be best served if we just dig into the actual financial performance of the company in recent years to see just how high quality the business is. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows have largely consistently improved during this window of time. And digging deeper, we can see where a lot of that growth has come from.

Perhaps the best place to start would be the largest segment, which involves its commercial aircraft. In the chart below, you can see revenue and segment profits for this unit over the last three fiscal years and for the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year. This year, this segment has come under some pressure on the bottom line. But the overall trend has been toward growth.

Management has benefited from an increase in the size of the company's order book. As order intake expanded over the years, totaling 4,247 aircraft from 2022 through the third quarter of this year, the total size of the order book expanded from 7,239 to 8,665. What's really remarkable here is that the company has been able to increase deliveries during this time as well. In 2022, the company delivered 661 aircraft. Last year, that number was 766. And for the first nine months of this year, the 507 aircraft delivered exceeded the 497 that it delivered for the same time last year. So what we have here is a business that is delivering more while simultaneously locking in even greater deliveries in years to come. This is the hallmark of a high-quality player.

This is not the only operating segment that has seen similar growth. The Airbus Helicopter segment has seen revenue expand from $7.89 billion in 2022 to $8.86 billion last year. And for the first nine months of this year, the $6.30 billion achieved by the unit beat out the $5.40 billion reported a year ago. And as was the case with the commercial portion of the business, a rise in revenue translated to an increase in profitability.

The company saw during this time a rise in the number of helicopters delivered from 344 to 361. And for the first nine months of this year, the 218 delivered exceeded the 190 delivered a year earlier. The firm has seen a rise in order intake, with growth from 362 in 2022 to 450 last year. And for the first nine months of this year, it saw 306 orders, which was down only a hair from the 308 reported a year ago. Order backlog is considerably lower here, amounting to only 981 aircraft in the most recent quarter. But that's up from the 757 that the firm ended 2022 with.

Perhaps the most problematic segment is the Airbus Defense and Space segment. Even though I find it to be the most fascinating because of my own personal interests, it has been plagued with profitability issues. Even as revenue increased, which you can see in the chart below, profitability has been inconsistent. In two of the last three years, it generated operating losses. Although for this year, the $412 million of profits did indicate that maybe the shoe is on the other foot now. Management provides less detail here. All we know is that from 2022 through 2024, the order intake for this unit expanded from 13,660 units up to 16,710 units. But this year, we have seen a drop, with the 6,753 units of intake falling short of the 10,971 experienced a year earlier. We don't have any data for this year yet when it comes to the total size of the order book for this unit. But we do know that, over the last three years, it expanded from 38,426 to 46,803.

Before I get to valuation, I do think it's worth noting that the company does report some backlog. But for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, management provided the total value of its order book for both its commercial aircraft and helicopter businesses. Combined, these grew from $411.23 billion to $582.99 billion. In all likelihood, we can expect this trend to continue. And the reason why I say this has to do with expectations for the industry over the next couple of decades.

As I detailed in a previous article about Boeing, the aviation space is ripe for growth. The management team at Airbus feels similarly. According to their own estimates, between 2025 and 2044, there should be demand for 42,450 new passenger aircraft alone. Right now, or I suppose I should say as of the end of 2024, there was a global fleet in the commercial aviation space of 24,730 aircraft.

But by 2044, the expected size of the fleet in service should balloon to 49,210. I know this implies a smaller increase than the 42,450 new passenger aircraft that management said will be needed. But the difference here comes from 18,930 of the 24,730 aircraft that will be needed replacing because of age and/or obsolescence. Presumably, and hopefully it's a very small number, some of this replacement will also come from crashes.

This expansion will be driven by an annualized global GDP growth rate of 2.5%. This would be fueled not only by continued productivity improvements, but also by an expansion in the global population amounting to 1.2 billion. Society will become more urbanized during this time, with the urban population expanding by 1.3 billion. And thanks to technology and a continued growth in world trade, the middle class will grow by an estimated 1.5 billion people. The wealthier society is, and the larger society is, the greater will be the demand for air travel, not only for personal travel and business travel, but also for the transportation of goods in what will become an increasingly globalized society.

Honestly, Airbus is well positioned here. The company boasts a 56% market share when it comes to single-aisle commercial aircraft. Specifically for the A320neo family, this amounts to 62%. And when it comes to the A321neo, it's 80%. When it comes to wide-body aircraft, the company boasts a 44% global market share. And when it comes to freighters, it's a whopping 52%. So if anybody is going to be a prime beneficiary of this expansion and the demand for air travel, it will be Airbus. Obviously, the other operating segments for the company will probably grow as well.

The Airbus Helicopter segment, for instance, expects total market upside from European defense spending to be €18 billion between 2025 and 2030. And even though its defense and space operations have been a laggard in terms of profitability, management expects the European defense market to boost the addressable market for air power by 9% on an annualized basis between 2025 and 2034. The connected intelligence market is expected to grow similarly, while the space systems market is expected to expand by 6% per annum. Management's hope is that, between 2024 and 2028, it can grow its EBITDA for this segment from a negative €656 million to a positive €1 billion.

When it comes to this year specifically, management is forecasting EBITDA of around $11.04 billion. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would get net profits of about $7.20 billion and adjusted operating cash flow of $10.54 billion. These would all represent drastic improvements over what the company saw in 2024. And honestly, they make a world of difference when it comes to the firm's valuation.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using these estimates, as well as using historical results for the 2024 fiscal year. Normally, these are the kinds of multiples that would justify a ‘hold’ rating in my book. But I provide extra wiggle room for high quality industry leaders, especially ones that have no net debt and instead boast a net cash position of $8.21 billion, like what Airbus has. In the same chart, you can see how the business stacks up against five other similar firms. On a price-to earnings basis, two of the five companies are cheaper than our candidate is. But when it comes to the other two trading multiples, only one of the five companies is cheaper.

Key risks to be aware of

As with any company, Airbus is also subject to risks. I would view the company-specific risks to be relatively limited. But there is one exception. And that relates to a recently discovered quality issue that the company revealed regarding metal panels on hundreds of its A320 aircraft. This emerged only days after warning that thousands of the jets that it has need an urgent software fix. According to the publicly known data, this seems to relate to a potential supplier issue whereby the thickness of some of the panels that sit on top of the cockpit and on either side of the right and left front doors of the jet are problematic. Basically, they are not thick enough, and will almost certainly need replacing. We have yet to know the full extent of this issue. But according to management, as many as 628 jets could have these defective panels installed.

This follows a well-publicized software glitch affecting roughly 6,000 of these jets. The good news is that the overwhelming majority of these have already been fixed. However, according to the company, the flight control computer on one of its jets malfunctioned because of intense solar radiation. The good news is that this was rather quick and easy to fix. But it does harken back to when Boeing aircraft crashed in late 2018 and early 2019, leading to a global grounding of its 737 Max models. That ended up proving incredibly costly for Boeing, and investors would be right to continue to monitor these types of quality control issues closely.

We also need to be cognizant of other risks that are outside of the company. As I detailed in other articles like here and here, the US economy seems to be barreling toward a recession. Tariffs have harmed the economy both directly and indirectly through the need to keep interest rates higher in order to combat inflationary pressures. The US is not the only area of risk for the company. There's also China, which has experienced significant challenges in recent memory.

As one source indicated, industrial output in China is growing slower than expected, with an expansion year over year of 4.9% in October compared to the 6.5% rise experienced the month before that. In the first 10 months of this year, fixed asset investment that includes Investments in real estate, dropped 1.7% year over year. This is the first drop in fixed asset investment that the country has seen since 2020, when it was negatively affected by the COVID-19 pandemic. And what makes matters worse is that conditions there are rapidly deteriorating. In October alone, fixed asset investment was down 11.4% compared to the same month last year. This indicates that the decline is accelerating. Manufacturing activity is contracting rather quickly also, and as I detailed in another article, consumer confidence in China has come under massive pressure in recent years.

I know these things might seem irrelevant to a company like Airbus. But if the two largest economies on the planet are struggling, it could mean a broader weakening of the global economy. Ultimately, that would lead to a reduction in demand for air travel, which would certainly prove to be prohibitive for growth for a firm such as this. And this is because of not only a decline associated with commercial trade, but passenger trade also.

Takeaway

Altogether, I must say that I am rather impressed with Airbus right now. The company has proven itself to be a high-quality operator, and it is benefiting from what should be a long-term trend of growth. Shares are decently priced and are certainly cheap compared to other similar firms. When you add on top of all of this a strong balance sheet, I do believe that rating it a ‘buy’ is the appropriate course of action here.

data.title: Tracking Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb's Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Ruane, Cunniff & Goldfarb’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 11/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb’s Portfolio article for an idea of their investment philosophy and our previous update for the moves during Q2 2025.

This quarter, Ruane, Cunniff, & Goldfarb’s 13F portfolio value increased from $5.83B to $6.04B. The number of holdings increased from 43 to 48. 18 of those stakes are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio each), and they are the focus of this article. The top three positions are at ~35%, while the top five are at ~51% of the 13F assets: Alphabet, Liberty Media, Charles Schwab, Taiwan Semi, and Elevance Health.

The firm is best known as the investment advisor of the Sequoia Fund (SEQUX), which has a venerable ~55-year track record (July 15, 1970, inception, 13.5% annualized return compared to 11.37% for the S&P 500 index). The performance for 2022 was terrible: Negative 30.52% compared to Negative 18.11% for the S&P 500 index. For 2023, they did slightly better than the S&P 500 Index: 27.83% vs. 26.29%. For 2024, they did 20.79% vs. ~25% for S&P. YTD through Q3 2025, they returned 21.59% compared to 14.83% for the S&P. The cash allocation, which had averaged ~20% since inception, is now less than 5%.

Note: The following significant stakes in Sequoia’s portfolio are not in the 13F report as they are not 13F securities: Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF), Eurofins Scientific SE (OTCPK:ERFSF), Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (OTCPK:RYCEY), SAP SE (SAP), Universal Music Group (OTCPK:UMGNF), and Ashtead Group plc (OTCPK:ASHTF). data.title: Rates Spark: Treasuries Ranging But Poised For A Breakout
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

We could be in this 4% to 4.1% trading range (US 10yr yield) for a bit before we break out. We'd view a break below as temporary (perhaps into the turn of the year), and a break above as more structural, certainly something for 2026.

Treasuries love that 4% to 4.1% trading range. Temporary break below more likely. But break above has more legs

The US ADP employment report printed negative for November, at -32k. It also printed negative in September, August and June this year, whereas there were no negative prints last year. Things have certainly slowed. But Treasuries have built a bit of resilience to the weak jobs narrative.

Partly because there are fewer immigrants coming into the country in net terms, requiring less employment generation. But also, it's productivity growth rather than employment growth driving things into the future (AI, among others).

The 10yr yield was lower ahead of the data, and despite the above caveats, this ADP number should have given it a further push lower. But instead it held (at around 4.06%). Import prices also came in for September (a bit of a lag) – very subdued; statistically zero (or close to) whether month-on-month or year-on-year.

At least the tariff risk is not being exacerbated by higher import prices. Export prices were up to 3.8% though – that's the highest rate in about three years, but nowhere near as high as the 15+% rates we saw in 2021/22. Overall, these are mostly second-tier data, and do not have a huge impact on levels. Industrial production data and ISM services came in too, but to little effect.

The issue here is a market lacking a good enough reason to break below 4%. And at the same time, there's a reluctance to push on higher above 4.1%. Friday's PCE data might give the sub-4% camp a boost, especially if we were to get a 0.1% MoM outcome (not impossible).

On the more likely 0.2% outcome, we'll then roll on into next week and the pre-FOMC run-in. The Challenger report on Thursday is another big one, potentially. But last month's big layoffs were, in the end, negated by low jobless claims right through that month.

We could be in this 4% to 4.1% trading range for the 10yr yield for a bit before we break out. We'd view a break below as temporary (perhaps into the turn of the year), and a break above as more structural (certainly something for 2026).

Thursday’s events and market view

Out of the eurozone, in terms of data, we will only get the retail sales figures for October. With Kocher, Lane, Cipollone and de Guindos, there are again a number of European Central Bank members scheduled to speak.

Key focus will remain on US job market indicators. We will get the Challenger job cuts numbers as well as the weekly jobless claims figures. The latter have so far painted a relatively benign picture, contrasting with the job cuts signalled by the ADP payrolls data, for instance. While the Fed is in its pre-meeting communication blackout, Bowman is still slated to talk about bank supervision and regulation.

In terms of issuance, Spain and France will conduct their final bond auctions for the year. Spain will sell 4y and 12y bonds as well as 11y inflation-linked bonds, for a total of up to €4.25bn overall. France sells longer bonds from 10y out to 30y maturities for a total of up to €5.5bn.
